flashcards.definition: Matter with definite shape and definite volume; particles vibrate in fixed positions.
Matter with definite volume but indefinite shape; particles slide past each other.
Matter with no definite shape or volume; particles move freely and fill containers.
An ionized gas of free electrons and ions; the most common state of matter in the universe.
A physically distinct form of matter (solid, liquid, gas, plasma).
Transition from solid to liquid; requires energy (endothermic).
Transition from liquid to solid; releases energy (exothermic).
Transition from liquid to gas at the surface; endothermic.
Transition from gas to liquid; exothermic.
Transition directly from solid to gas without passing through the liquid phase.
A property that does NOT depend on the amount of substance (density, color, boiling point).
A property that DOES depend on the amount of substance (mass, volume, length).
Mass per unit volume (d = m/v); an intensive property.
The temperature at which a liquid becomes a gas; an intensive property.
The temperature at which a solid becomes a liquid; an intensive property.
d = m/v; mass divided by volume. Units: g/mL or g/cm³.
1.00 g/mL at 4°C — the standard reference for liquid density.
An object floats if its density is less than the fluid; sinks if greater.
Measuring an irregular object's volume by the water it displaces.
Common units: g/mL, g/cm³, kg/m³. 1 g/mL = 1 g/cm³.
A mixture with visibly different components throughout (trail mix, salad).
A mixture with uniform composition throughout; also called a solution (salt water).
A homogeneous mixture of a solute dissolved in a solvent.
A mixture with particles that are larger than in a solution but don't settle (milk, fog).
A mixture with large particles that eventually settle out (muddy water).
A pure substance made of one type of atom; listed on the periodic table.
A substance made of two or more elements chemically bonded in a fixed ratio.
Two or more substances physically combined; can be separated by physical means.
Matter with a constant composition — either an element or a compound.
Separating mixtures using physical methods: filtration, distillation, evaporation.
A change in form or appearance without changing the substance's identity (melting ice).
A change that produces a new substance with different properties (burning, rusting).
Color change, gas production, precipitate formation, temperature/energy change, odor change.
Physical changes are usually reversible; chemical changes are usually irreversible.
Mass is neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
Energy of motion; increases with temperature as particles move faster.
Stored energy due to position or chemical bonds.
A process that absorbs heat from surroundings (melting, evaporation).
A process that releases heat to surroundings (freezing, condensation, combustion).
Total kinetic energy of all particles in a substance; proportional to temperature.
pH = −log[H⁺]; if [H⁺] = 10⁻³ M then pH = 3.
pOH = −log[OH⁻]; if [OH⁻] = 10⁻⁵ M then pOH = 5.
A change of 1 pH unit = a 10-fold change in [H⁺].
A substance that donates H⁺ ions (Arrhenius) or protons (Brønsted-Lowry) in solution.
A substance that donates OH⁻ ions (Arrhenius) or accepts protons (Brønsted-Lowry).
Acid: produces H⁺ in water. Base: produces OH⁻ in water.
Acid: proton (H⁺) donor. Base: proton acceptor.
A substance that can act as both an acid and a base (e.g., water).
An acid with only hydrogen and one other element (e.g., HCl, HF, H₂S).
An acid containing hydrogen, oxygen, and another element (e.g., HNO₃, H₂SO₄).
Prefix 'hydro-' + root of element + '-ic acid' (HCl → hydrochloric acid).
Based on the polyatomic ion: -ate → -ic acid; -ite → -ous acid.
HNO₃ (nitric), H₂SO₄ (sulfuric), H₃PO₄ (phosphoric), HClO₃ (chloric).
Binary: hydro___ic acid. Oxyacid: ___ic or ___ous acid based on the ion.
H₂SO₄ — a strong diprotic oxyacid; widely used in industry.
HCl — a strong binary acid; found in stomach acid.
HNO₃ — a strong oxyacid used in fertilizers and explosives.
CH₃COOH — a weak organic acid; the main component of vinegar.
Blue litmus turns red in acid; red litmus turns blue in base.
An indicator that is colorless in acid and pink in base.
Acids taste sour; bases taste bitter and feel slippery.
Acids and bases conduct electricity in solution because they produce ions.
A substance that changes color depending on the pH of a solution.
Completely ionizes in water (HCl, HNO₃, H₂SO₄, HBr, HI, HClO₄).
Partially ionizes in water; establishes equilibrium (CH₃COOH, HF, H₂CO₃).
Completely dissociates in water (NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂).
Partially ionizes in water (NH₃, CH₃NH₂).
Strong: ~100% ionized. Weak: <100% ionized; most remains molecular.
Acid + Base → Salt + Water (HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O).
An ionic compound formed from the cation of a base and the anion of an acid.
H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l) — the essence of neutralization.
A lab technique to determine the concentration of an acid or base using a known solution.
The point in a titration where moles of acid equal moles of base.
Name the cation (from base) + anion (from acid): NaOH + HCl → sodium chloride.
Table salt; from NaOH + HCl neutralization.
A salt from Ca(OH)₂ + H₂SO₄; used in plaster.
A salt from KOH + HNO₃; used in fertilizers and fireworks.
A salt from NH₃ + HCl; used in dry cell batteries.
A solution that resists changes in pH when small amounts of acid or base are added.
A weak acid and its conjugate base (or a weak base and its conjugate acid).
The weak acid neutralizes added base; conjugate base neutralizes added acid.
The amount of acid or base a buffer can neutralize before pH changes significantly.
Blood uses carbonic acid–bicarbonate buffer to maintain pH ≈ 7.4.
Measures acidity: 0–6.9 acidic, 7 neutral, 7.1–14 basic.
−log[H⁺]; lower pH = more acidic.
−log[OH⁻]; lower pOH = more basic.
At 25°C, the sum of pH and pOH always equals 14.
Energy transferred between objects due to a temperature difference; measured in joules or calories.
The energy needed to raise 1 g of water by 1°C. 1 cal = 4.184 J.
The SI unit of energy. 1 kJ = 1000 J.
Temperature: average KE of particles. Heat: total energy transferred.
Convert between J, kJ, cal, and kcal using: 1 cal = 4.184 J, 1 kcal = 4184 J.
The amount of heat needed to raise 1 g of a substance by 1°C (J/g·°C).
4.184 J/g·°C — very high, which moderates Earth's climate.
Heat = mass × specific heat × temperature change.
Absorbs a lot of heat with little temperature change (water, metals resist temp change less).
Solve for any variable: q (heat), m (mass), c (specific heat), or ΔT.
The amount of heat needed to raise the temperature of an object by 1°C (J/°C).
Heat capacity = mass × specific heat.
Specific heat is per gram; heat capacity is for the whole object.
Heat capacity per mole of substance (J/mol·°C).
Objects with high heat capacity (oceans) change temperature slowly.
The measurement of heat changes during chemical or physical processes.
An insulated device used to measure heat exchange in a reaction.
A simple constant-pressure calorimeter used for reactions in solution.
A constant-volume calorimeter for measuring energy in combustion reactions.
In a calorimeter: q_hot + q_cold = 0 (conservation of energy).
The total heat content of a system at constant pressure.
ΔH < 0: exothermic. ΔH > 0: endothermic.
A measure of disorder or randomness in a system; tends to increase.
ΔG = ΔH − TΔS; ΔG < 0 means a reaction is spontaneous.
A reaction that occurs without external energy input (ΔG < 0).
The total enthalpy change is the same regardless of the pathway (if same initial and final states).
ΔH°f: the enthalpy change when 1 mole of a compound forms from its elements in standard states.
Add, reverse, or multiply given reactions to match the target reaction; adjust ΔH accordingly.
A visual showing reactant and product energy levels and ΔH for each step.
Calculate ΔH for reactions that are difficult to measure directly.
Splitting a heavy nucleus into lighter nuclei, releasing enormous energy (nuclear power).
Combining light nuclei into a heavier nucleus; releases even more energy (powers the sun).
A self-sustaining series of fission events; each fission triggers more.
The minimum amount of fissile material needed to sustain a chain reaction.
E = mc²; mass is converted to energy in nuclear reactions.
Nucleus emits an alpha particle (⁴₂He); mass number decreases by 4, atomic number by 2.
A neutron converts to a proton, emitting a beta particle (electron); atomic number increases by 1.
High-energy electromagnetic radiation emitted after alpha or beta decay; no change in mass or charge.
Alpha < Beta < Gamma; alpha stopped by paper, beta by aluminum, gamma by lead.
Radiation with enough energy to remove electrons from atoms (alpha, beta, gamma).
Shows the decay of a nucleus: the sum of mass numbers and atomic numbers must balance.
Conversion of one element into another through nuclear reactions.
Total mass numbers equal on both sides; total atomic numbers equal on both sides.
A proton converts to a neutron, emitting a positron (e⁺); atomic number decreases by 1.
An inner electron is captured by the nucleus; a proton becomes a neutron.
The time for half of a radioactive sample to decay; constant for each isotope.
N = N₀ × (1/2)ⁿ where n = number of half-lives.
n = total time / half-life.
A graph showing exponential decrease of a radioactive sample over time.
Carbon-14 dating uses half-life (5,730 years) to date organic materials.
Fission of U-235 in reactors generates electricity; produces radioactive waste.
Radioisotopes like Tc-99m are used in medical scans (PET, SPECT).
Using C-14 decay to determine the age of organic remains (up to ~50,000 years).
Using gamma rays to kill bacteria and extend shelf life of food.
Using targeted radiation to destroy cancer cells.
Expressing numbers as a × 10ⁿ where 1 ≤ a < 10.
The number 'a' in scientific notation (must be 1–9.999…).
The power of 10 in scientific notation; positive for large, negative for small numbers.
The regular decimal notation of a number (e.g., 602,000,000,000,000,000,000,000).
The power of 10 closest to a quantity; used for quick comparisons.
All digits known with certainty plus one estimated digit in a measurement.
All non-zero digits are significant.
Zeros before the first non-zero digit are NOT significant (0.0045 has 2 sig figs).
Zeros after a decimal point ARE significant (2.500 has 4 sig figs).
The answer has the same number of sig figs as the least precise measurement.
How close a measurement is to the true (accepted) value.
How close repeated measurements are to each other.
|(Experimental − Accepted) / Accepted| × 100%.
A consistent, repeatable error in the same direction (miscalibrated instrument).
Irregular, unpredictable deviations that average out over many measurements.
The International System of Units — the standard system of measurement in science.
SI prefix meaning 1,000 (10³). 1 kg = 1,000 g.
SI prefix meaning 1/100 (10⁻²). 1 cm = 0.01 m.
SI prefix meaning 1/1,000 (10⁻³). 1 mL = 0.001 L.
Fundamental SI units: meter (m), kilogram (kg), second (s), kelvin (K), mole (mol).
A problem-solving method using conversion factors to switch between units.
A ratio equal to 1 used to convert units (e.g., 1 km / 1000 m).
Arranging conversion factors so unwanted units cancel out.
Chaining multiple conversion factors to go from one unit to another.
A unit formed by combining base units (e.g., m/s for speed, kg/m³ for density).
A positively charged subatomic particle in the nucleus; defines the element (atomic number).
A neutral subatomic particle in the nucleus; contributes to mass number.
A negatively charged particle orbiting the nucleus; determines bonding and chemistry.
The number of protons in the nucleus of an atom; identifies the element.
The total number of protons + neutrons in an atom's nucleus.
The dense central core of an atom containing protons and neutrons.
The region around the nucleus where electrons are likely to be found.
A fixed region around the nucleus where electrons with a specific energy are found (n = 1, 2, 3…).
The distance from the nucleus to the outermost electron; decreases across a period.
An atom that has gained or lost electrons, giving it a net electric charge.
A three-dimensional region around the nucleus where an electron is most likely found.
A spherical orbital that holds up to 2 electrons; exists in every energy level.
A dumbbell-shaped orbital; three per energy level (starting at n = 2), holding up to 6 electrons.
It's impossible to simultaneously know both the exact position and momentum of an electron.
Four numbers (n, l, mₗ, mₛ) that describe the state of an electron in an atom.
The range of all EM radiation: radio, microwave, IR, visible, UV, X-ray, gamma.
The distance between successive wave crests; measured in nm or m.
The number of wave crests passing a point per second; measured in Hz.
A particle (quantum) of electromagnetic radiation; energy E = hν.
The set of specific wavelengths emitted by an excited element — a chemical fingerprint.
The spontaneous emission of particles or energy from an unstable nucleus.
A nucleus is stable when the strong nuclear force overcomes proton-proton repulsion.
The region on a neutron-vs-proton graph where stable nuclei are found.
The process by which an unstable nucleus loses energy by emitting radiation.
The strong force that holds protons and neutrons together in the nucleus.
Atoms of the same element with different numbers of neutrons (same Z, different A).
An isotope with an unstable nucleus that undergoes radioactive decay.
The weighted average mass of all naturally occurring isotopes of an element (in amu).
An instrument that measures the masses and relative abundances of isotopes.
A radioisotope used in radiocarbon dating of organic remains (half-life ≈ 5,730 years).
A device that accelerates charged particles to high speeds, used to create new elements.
An element with atomic number greater than 92 (uranium); all are synthetic.
Combining light nuclei to form a heavier nucleus; powers stars.
The formation of elements in stars through nuclear reactions.
Elements beyond Z = 103; created in labs, very unstable, short-lived.
All matter is made of indivisible atoms; atoms of the same element are identical.
The 'plum pudding' model — electrons embedded in a positive sphere.
Discovered the dense, positive nucleus via the gold foil experiment (1911).
Electrons orbit the nucleus in fixed energy levels; explained hydrogen spectrum.
Current model: electrons exist in probability orbitals, not fixed orbits.
A one- or two-letter abbreviation for an element (e.g., Na, Cl, Fe).
A tabular arrangement of elements by increasing atomic number and recurring properties.
A vertical column on the periodic table; elements share similar chemical properties.
A horizontal row on the periodic table; properties change systematically across.
Writing an element with its mass number and atomic number (e.g., ¹²₆C).
Elements that are shiny, malleable, ductile, and good conductors; left/center of table.
Elements that are dull, brittle, poor conductors; upper right of table.
Elements with properties between metals and nonmetals (B, Si, Ge, As, Sb, Te, Po).
Group 1: highly reactive metals with 1 valence electron (Li, Na, K, etc.).
Group 18: very stable, unreactive gases with full valence shells (He, Ne, Ar, etc.).
An electron in the outermost energy level; determines an element's reactivity.
Atoms tend to gain, lose, or share electrons to have 8 valence electrons.
Increases down a group (easier to lose electrons).
Increases up a group and to the right (easier to gain electrons).
Group 17 elements; very reactive nonmetals with 7 valence electrons (F, Cl, Br, I).
Divisions within energy levels: s, p, d, f with increasing energy.
Holds up to 2 electrons in 1 orbital; present in all energy levels.
Holds up to 6 electrons in 3 orbitals; starts at n = 2.
Holds up to 10 electrons in 5 orbitals; starts at n = 3.
Holds up to 14 electrons in 7 orbitals; starts at n = 4.
The arrangement of electrons in an atom's orbitals (e.g., 1s² 2s² 2p⁶ 3s¹ for Na).
Electrons fill the lowest energy orbitals first.
No two electrons in an atom can have all four quantum numbers identical (max 2 per orbital).
Electrons fill orbitals of equal energy singly before pairing up.
Writing electron configuration using the preceding noble gas (e.g., [Ne] 3s¹ for Na).
The energy needed to remove an electron; increases across a period, decreases down a group.
The ability of an atom to attract electrons in a bond; increases across and up (F is highest).
The size of an atom; increases down a group, decreases across a period.
The energy change when an electron is added to a neutral atom.
A pattern in element properties that repeats across periods and down groups.
Inner electrons block outer electrons from the full nuclear charge.
The net positive charge experienced by valence electrons; Zeff = Z − shielding.
Inner electrons that shield valence electrons from the nucleus.
Attraction between nucleus and electron increases with charge, decreases with distance.
Atoms get smaller across a period because Zeff increases while shielding stays roughly constant.
Valence Shell Electron Pair Repulsion: electron pairs around a central atom arrange to minimize repulsion.
Molecular shape with a 180° bond angle (e.g., CO₂, BeCl₂).
4 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → bond angles of 109.5° (e.g., CH₄).
3 bonding pairs, 0 lone pairs → bond angles of 120° (e.g., BF₃).
A nonlinear shape due to lone pairs on the central atom (e.g., H₂O, 104.5°).
Spherical; one per energy level.
Dumbbell-shaped; three per energy level (px, py, pz), mutually perpendicular.
Cloverleaf or donut-belt shapes; five per energy level starting at n = 3.
Complex multi-lobed shapes; seven per energy level starting at n = 4.
A region where the probability of finding an electron is zero.
Two or more reactants combine to form one product: A + B → AB.
One compound breaks down into two or more simpler substances: AB → A + B.
An element replaces another in a compound: A + BC → AC + B.
Two compounds exchange ions: AB + CD → AD + CB.
A ranking of elements by their ability to displace others in single replacement reactions.
A substance reacts with oxygen, producing heat and light. Hydrocarbon → CO₂ + H₂O.
All fuel burns in excess O₂; products are CO₂ and H₂O.
Insufficient O₂; produces CO (carbon monoxide) or C (soot).
An organic compound containing only hydrogen and carbon (e.g., CH₄, C₃H₈).
Combustion always releases energy (exothermic), which is why fuels are useful.
Loss of electrons (increase in oxidation number). 'OIL' — Oxidation Is Loss.
Gain of electrons (decrease in oxidation number). 'RIG' — Reduction Is Gain.
The substance that is reduced (gains electrons); causes oxidation in others.
The substance that is oxidized (loses electrons); causes reduction in others.
A number indicating the apparent charge on an atom in a compound.
The minimum energy required for reactant molecules to undergo a chemical reaction.
A substance that lowers activation energy and speeds up a reaction without being consumed.
The highest-energy arrangement of atoms during a reaction; unstable intermediate.
A graph showing energy changes during a reaction (reactants → transition state → products).
Exothermic: products lower energy than reactants. Endothermic: products higher.
A chemical equation with equal numbers of each atom on both sides.
A number placed before a formula in a balanced equation to indicate the number of units.
In a chemical reaction, total mass of reactants equals total mass of products.
A number in a chemical formula indicating the number of atoms of an element (H₂O: 2 H's).
1) Write unbalanced equation. 2) Count atoms. 3) Add coefficients. 4) Verify.
The speed at which reactants are consumed or products are formed.
Reactions occur when particles collide with enough energy and correct orientation.
Increasing temperature increases kinetic energy and collision frequency → faster reaction.
Higher concentration means more particles, more collisions, faster reaction.
Smaller particle size → greater surface area → faster reaction.
A state where forward and reverse reactions occur at equal rates; concentrations stay constant.
If a system at equilibrium is stressed, it shifts to partially counteract the stress.
A reaction that can proceed in both forward and reverse directions: A + B ⇌ C + D.
The ratio of product concentrations to reactant concentrations at equilibrium.
Adding reactant → shifts right. Adding product → shifts left. Temperature/pressure also cause shifts.
A positively charged ion (lost electrons); metals typically form cations (Na⁺, Ca²⁺).
A negatively charged ion (gained electrons); nonmetals typically form anions (Cl⁻, O²⁻).
A technique for writing formulas: swap the charges of the ions as subscripts.
A charged group of covalently bonded atoms (e.g., SO₄²⁻, NH₄⁺, NO₃⁻).
A representation showing the types and numbers of atoms in a compound (H₂O, NaCl).
The mass of one mole of a substance, in grams per mole (g/mol).
The sum of atomic masses of all atoms in a molecular formula.
The sum of atomic masses for an ionic compound's formula unit.
One-twelfth the mass of a carbon-12 atom; ~1.66 × 10⁻²⁴ g.
Add up the atomic masses (from periodic table) of all atoms in the formula.
6.022 × 10²³ — the number of particles in one mole of a substance.
The SI unit for amount of substance; equals 6.022 × 10²³ particles.
Multiply moles by 6.022 × 10²³.
Divide number of particles by 6.022 × 10²³.
12.00 g of C-12; 6.022 × 10²³ atoms, molecules, or formula units.
Multiply moles by molar mass (g/mol).
Divide grams by molar mass.
Multiply moles by 22.4 L/mol.
Standard Temperature and Pressure: 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm.
At STP, one mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 L.
The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound (e.g., CH₂O for glucose).
The actual number of atoms in a molecule (e.g., C₆H₁₂O₆ for glucose).
The percentage by mass of each element in a compound.
Convert % to grams → moles → divide by smallest → simplify to whole numbers.
Divide molar mass by empirical formula mass to find the multiplier.
The reactant completely consumed first; determines the maximum yield of product.
The reactant that remains after the limiting reagent is used up.
The maximum amount of product predicted by stoichiometry from the limiting reagent.
Convert both reactants to moles of product; the one giving less product is limiting.
The mole ratio of reactants and products from the balanced equation.
Calculations relating amounts of reactants and products in a chemical reaction.
The ratio of moles of one substance to another from the balanced equation coefficients.
Convert given mass → moles → use mole ratio → moles of target → mass.
Provide the mole ratios needed for stoichiometric conversions.
Use conversion factors: molar mass and mole ratios to solve problems.
(Actual yield / Theoretical yield) × 100%.
The amount of product actually obtained from an experiment.
The maximum predicted product from stoichiometry.
Incomplete reactions, side reactions, transfer losses, impure reagents.
Rarely achieved; actual yield is almost always less than theoretical.
A gas consisting of single atoms (He, Ne, Ar — noble gases).
A gas whose molecules consist of two atoms (H₂, O₂, N₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂).
The seven diatomic elements: H₂, N₂, O₂, F₂, Cl₂, Br₂, I₂ (BrINClHOF).
A hypothetical gas whose particles have no volume and no intermolecular forces.
Gases expand to fill containers, are compressible, and exert pressure.
Force per unit area; measured in atm, kPa, mmHg, or psi.
1 atm = 101.325 kPa = 760 mmHg = 760 torr.
An instrument that measures atmospheric pressure using a column of mercury.
A device that measures the pressure of an enclosed gas relative to atmospheric pressure.
Standard Temperature and Pressure: 0°C (273.15 K) and 1 atm.
Gas particles are in constant random motion; collisions are perfectly elastic.
Average kinetic energy of gas particles is directly proportional to absolute temperature (K).
A collision where total kinetic energy is conserved (no energy lost).
Gas particles move in straight lines in all directions until they collide.
Negligible compared to the volume of the container (ideal gas assumption).
P₁V₁ = P₂V₂; at constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely proportional.
As one variable increases, the other decreases (P ↑ → V ↓).
Boyle's Law requires temperature to remain unchanged (isothermal).
Decreasing volume increases pressure (more collisions per unit area).
Syringes, breathing mechanics, hydraulic systems.
V₁/T₁ = V₂/T₂; at constant pressure, volume is directly proportional to temperature (K).
As temperature increases, volume increases (V ↑ when T ↑).
0 K (−273.15°C) — the theoretical temperature where gas volume would be zero.
The absolute temperature scale; K = °C + 273.15. Must use Kelvin in gas laws.
Hot air balloons rise because heated air expands, becoming less dense.
P₁/T₁ = P₂/T₂; at constant volume, pressure is directly proportional to temperature (K).
The container's volume doesn't change (e.g., a rigid sealed container).
Heating a gas in a rigid container increases pressure (more energetic collisions).
Pressure cookers, tire pressure increasing on hot days.
Aerosol cans can explode if heated — Gay-Lussac's Law in action.
(P₁V₁)/T₁ = (P₂V₂)/T₂; combines Boyle's, Charles', and Gay-Lussac's laws.
Use when pressure, volume, AND temperature all change.
Hold one variable constant to get Boyle's (T), Charles' (P), or Gay-Lussac's (V).
T must be in Kelvin; P and V can be any consistent units.
Identify which variables change, plug in known values, solve for the unknown.
PV = nRT; relates pressure, volume, moles, and temperature of an ideal gas.
0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K) or 8.314 J/(mol·K).
The amount of gas in moles.
Calculate any one variable (P, V, n, or T) if the other three are known.
Works well at high T and low P; real gases deviate at high P and low T.
An actual gas whose behavior deviates from the ideal gas law at high P or low T.
Attractions between gas molecules; cause real gases to occupy less volume than predicted.
At high pressures, the actual volume of gas particles becomes significant.
(P + a/V²)(V − b) = nRT — corrects for intermolecular forces (a) and particle volume (b).
At high temperatures and low pressures.
The substance being dissolved (usually in smaller amount).
The substance doing the dissolving (usually in larger amount — often water).
A solution where water is the solvent.
The process of a solute dissolving in a solvent.
Polar solvents dissolve polar/ionic solutes; nonpolar dissolves nonpolar.
The amount of solute dissolved in a given amount of solution.
Moles of solute per liter of solution: M = mol/L.
(mass of solute / mass of solution) × 100%.
(mass of solute / mass of solution) × 10⁶; used for very dilute solutions.
Concentrated: high solute ratio. Dilute: low solute ratio.
Adding solvent to reduce the concentration of a solution.
M₁V₁ = M₂V₂; moles of solute remain constant during dilution.
A concentrated solution that will be diluted to a lower concentration.
A series of successive dilutions, each reducing concentration by a fixed ratio.
Making lab solutions at desired concentrations from concentrated stock.
Moles of solute per liter of solution (mol/L); most common concentration unit in chemistry.
M = mol of solute / V of solution (in liters).
mol = M × V (liters).
V = mol / M.
Convert volume + molarity to moles, then use mole ratios.
Contains the maximum amount of solute that can dissolve at a given temperature.
Contains less solute than the maximum; more can dissolve.
Contains more dissolved solute than normally possible; unstable — crystallizes if disturbed.
The maximum amount of solute that dissolves in a given amount of solvent at a specific temperature.
Guidelines predicting whether ionic compounds dissolve in water (e.g., all Na⁺ salts are soluble).
Solubility of solids generally increases with temperature; gases decrease.
Henry's Law: gas solubility increases with pressure (e.g., CO₂ in soda).
Stirring increases the rate of dissolving (exposes fresh solvent to solute).
Smaller solute particles dissolve faster (greater surface area).
Polar-polar and nonpolar-nonpolar pairs dissolve well; unlike pairs do not.
A property that depends on the number of solute particles, not their identity.
Adding solute raises the boiling point of a solvent.
Adding solute lowers the freezing point of a solvent (road salt on ice).
Pressure needed to stop osmosis across a semipermeable membrane.
Solute particles reduce the rate of evaporation, lowering vapor pressure.
A graph showing the solubility of a substance vs. temperature.
Points on the curve = saturated; below = unsaturated; above = supersaturated.
Most solids: solubility increases with temperature (upward-sloping curve).
Some salts (e.g., Ce₂(SO₄)₃): solubility decreases with temperature.
Gas solubility always decreases with increasing temperature.
